bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the House vote as a risky, go-it-alone bid to fund the Iran war and advance “Trump’s priorities,” using skeptical phrases like “long-shot” and “uncertain” to spotlight partisan maneuvering and Senate trouble.

Right-Leaning coverage, by contrast, casts the same $95 billion package as a strategic win, stressing “defense funding,” “filibuster-proof” muscle, and election-integrity or voter-ID provisions, while warning of “midterm election fears” and an “unpopular conflict.” Center-leaning reporting sits between them, emphasizing procedure, the close 216-214 margin, and concerns over no offsetting cuts.
All three agree the plan is Republican-only, war-linked, and Senate-bound.
summary: On Wednesday, the House adopted a $95 billion budget proposal by a 216-214 vote, with all Democrats opposed to the Republican-only measure aimed at funding the Iran war and President Donald Trump 's priorities.

Speaker Mike Johnson is using the budget reconciliation process to bypass the Senate filibuster, addressing funding pressures after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth reported the Pentagon is running short of cash despite a $150 billion allotment last year.

The package allocates $60 billion for the Pentagon, $13 billion for national security, $12 billion for farmers, and $10 billion for voting law changes aligned with the SAVE America Act. Opponents warn proof-of-citizenship rules could disenfranchise eligible Americans.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune signaled he may hold the House resolution as a potential fallback to keep government funded if negotiations fail, though internal GOP division over missing budget offsets complicates passage.

With current funds expiring Sept 30, Republicans navigate conflicting spending demands as some lawmakers argue Congress needs a "much higher level of involvement" in ending the war before the midterm elections.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the bill as a dangerous escalation, using phrases like “tees up billions,” “Iran war,” and “Israeli military integration” to spotlight war-making, executive overreach, and collapsing “bipartisan consensus.” Right-leaning coverage instead centers passage, military strength, and procedure, stressing “narrowly passes,” “defense policy bill,” and even “Department of War” renaming, while treating Iran and Israel as security concerns rather than broader ideological alarms.

The key dividing lines are war powers, Pentagon ties to Israel, and the bill’s huge $1.15 trillion scale; both sides agree the vote was narrow and contentious, but they invert whether that signals reckless expansion or necessary defense.
summary: On Wednesday, the U.S. House of Representatives narrowly passed a $1.15 trillion defense policy bill by a 216-212 vote, advancing the fiscal 2027 National Defense Authorization Act despite deep partisan divisions over its scope and provisions.

Democrats objected to the bill's massive cost and provisions that would expand Pentagon ties to Israel, while raising concerns about escalation risks with Iran as the conflict stretches into its fifth month.

The bill authorizes pay raises up to 7% for ranks E-5 and below, while President Donald Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth honored Army Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad of the 57th Air Defense Artillery Regiment during a dignified transfer Wednesday at Dover Air Force Base, Delaware.

Republican support enabled the bill to advance from the House, with the authorization funding defense systems development and military equipment as the Trump administration pursues its defense modernization agenda.

The razor-thin margin reflects deep partisan divisions over defense spending and foreign policy, with Senate Democrats expected to mount renewed opposition as the measure advances through reconciliation and faces disputes over Iran war costs and Israel military cooperation.
title: US House passes $1 trillion defense bill despite Iran, Israel concerns

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the pact as a Trump-era “policy shift,” stressing commercial upside for U.S. firms and warning that it “cracks the door open” to Saudi enrichment, with congressional review under the Atomic Energy Act as a pivotal brake.

Right-Leaning coverage treats it more as a strategic “deal” and “geopolitical game,” adding procedural detail about the Section 123 agreement, signatories, and assurances about the “highest standards of safety,” while still nodding to fears of “nuclear armament.” Center coverage is more detached, but it still casts the agreement as a “triumph” for the Saudi crown prince amid “war and uncertainty.” Both sides agree it is civilian nuclear cooperation, yet diverge over whether it signals opportunity, risk, or power politics .
summary: U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright and Saudi Energy Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman signed a landmark peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement, known as a 123 Agreement, alongside a bilateral safeguards agreement.

The 30-year, multibillion-dollar deal creates a framework for American companies, including Westinghouse Electric, to build civilian reactors and develop Saudi Arabia 's nuclear energy infrastructure.
The pact opens the door for potential uranium enrichment on Saudi soil following a joint feasibility study, provided operations are managed under U.S. oversight and contracted controls.

Non-Proliferation experts and critics voiced concern that the agreement omits traditional "gold standard" guardrails, such as explicit bans on domestic enrichment or mandatory adoption of the IAEA’s Additional Protocol.
The agreement now goes to the U.S. Congress for a 90-day review period under the Atomic Energy Act, where lawmakers can examine the terms or introduce a joint resolution of disapproval.
title: US and Saudi Arabia sign new nuclear agreement that could pave way for kingdom to enrich uranium

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Biggs as a polarizing, “right-wing” Trump figure, spotlighting “election-denying” and “falsely claim” language to make ideology and 2020 denial the story; right-leaning coverage instead celebrates a GOP win with upbeat, combative terms like “firebrand,” “wins,” “showdown,” and “BREAKING,” treating the primary as momentum for conservatives.
Center coverage is more procedural, stressing the matchup and battleground context.
The key dividing lines are Trump loyalty, election denial, and whether a 71.5% win signals extremism or strength.
Both sides agree Biggs won, beat Schweikert, and faces Hobbs, but they frame Arizona as either a warning sign or a political opening.
summary: On Tuesday, U.S. Rep. Andy Biggs won the Republican nomination for Arizona governor, defeating fellow Rep. David Schweikert with over 71% of the vote and advancing to a competitive November general election against incumbent Democratic Gov. Katie Hobbs .

President Donald Trump's endorsement and support from Turning Point Action's grassroots turnout machine proved decisive, while Schweikert struggled to raise money and could not overcome Biggs' momentum despite his fiscal-conservative alternative pitch.

Democrats plan to portray Biggs as an extremist, citing his tenure as chair of the House Freedom Caucus from 2019 to 2022 and his vote against certifying Arizona's 2020 election results after the Jan. 6, 2021 riot.

Biggs' nomination poses a strategic challenge for Arizona Republicans, who have lost recent statewide races after nominating Trump loyalists like Kari Lake four years ago, raising questions about independent voter appeal.

Maricopa County officials reported power outages at two polling sites on Tuesday, with connectivity issues affecting electronic poll books earlier in the day, underscoring election scrutiny in a county managing two-thirds of Arizona's voters.
title: Andy Biggs wins Republican nomination to challenge Democrat Katie Hobbs for Arizona governor

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the vote as a move to head off an election-season shutdown, stressing political stakes, pressure on Senate Democrats, and broader GOP priorities like Iran war funding, farm aid, and voter ID requirements; right-leaning coverage instead spotlights Republican strategy, using sharper terms like “punt,” “battle,” and “showdown” to portray the House as delaying conflict until after the midterms.
The dividing line is whether the bill is a pragmatic stopgap or a tactical deferral, with both sides noting the 220-205 party-line vote and the Senate’s uncertain rewrite.
Shared emphasis on avoiding shutdowns reflects a broader fight over governance, timing, and partisan leverage.
summary: On Tuesday, the House voted 220 to 205 to extend government funding through Dec. 4, aiming to avert a shutdown before the November midterm elections.

House Appropriations Committee Chairman Tom Cole said the clean extension avoids disruptions as the funding process has stalled in the Senate with no annual appropriations bills cleared by the committee.

Democratic leaders opposed the measure, stating it "fails to block funding for Donald Trump 's violent mass deportation machine" and grants the administration excessive budget authority without reforms.
Senate Majority Leader John Thune said he would put the funding plan to a vote before the August recess, indicating he may use reconciliation to bypass Democratic opposition.

The legislative effort follows two government shutdowns earlier this Congress, with leaders hoping this short-term patch provides stability while avoiding partisan funding battles seen in previous months.
title: House passes bill to fund the government until after midterm elections

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the $37.5 billion figure as a wasteful Trump-era “blown” budget hole, using skeptical, adversarial language like “grilled,” “contentious,” and “suspiciously low estimate” to spotlight accountability and even legality, while right-leaning coverage presents the same sum as a serious “funding request” that requires scrutiny but may be an “urgent” or “necessary” investment in readiness and deterrence.
The biggest dividing lines are whether the war is a scandal, a “quagmire,” or a budget pressure problem, and whether protests and senators’ questions signal justified alarm or disruptive hostility.
Both sides agree on the cost and hearing, but diverge sharply on what those facts mean.
summary: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told Congress the Iran war has cost an estimated $37.5 billion so far and requested an additional $67 billion for military funding, including critical needs such as military pay and equipment supplies.
Senators questioned Hegseth extensively about the lack of a clear plan to end the Iran war and why unspent funds from last year's Pentagon budget, totaling $150 billion, were not used.

Some senators criticized parts of the funding request as unrelated to the Iran war, mentioning allocations for National Guard patrols in Washington, D.C., U.S.-Mexico border troops, and drug trafficking operations.
Hegseth defended the necessity of the budget request and accused Democrats of political gamesmanship, while senators warned the U.S. risks sliding into 'another forever war'.
title: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth tells Congress that the latest estimate of the cost of the Iran war is $37.5 billion

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the findings as a public- health warning, using words like “linked,” “could cause,” and “quality of life” to spotlight personal harm and a stronger causal implication.
Right-Leaning coverage, by contrast, de-emphasizes alarm and leans on “rare, but real” plus specific odds—“37% higher,” “68% higher”—to situate the risk as limited and comparative.
The key dividing line is whether hair loss is portrayed as a notable downside of transformative drugs or a low-probability side effect.
Both sides agree it exists and is uncommon, reflecting shared medical context but different thresholds for concern.
summary: On Wednesday, a study published in The BMJ linked GLP-1 drugs—including Wegovy, Ozempic, Mounjaro, and Zepbound—to a higher risk of alopecia in type 2 diabetes patients compared to SGLT-2 and DPP-4 inhibitors.
Researchers at the University of Pennsylvania Health System reported a 37% higher risk of hair loss for GLP-1 users compared to SGLT-2 inhibitors, and a 68% higher risk versus DPP-4 inhibitors.
While the risk remains low at roughly 7 people per 1,000 annually, the team cited "reduced caloric intake" as the "most likely mechanism" disrupting hair growth cycles.

The US Food and Drug Administration is evaluating the safety signal, while the UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency has received 399 reports of hair loss linked to tirzepatide in 2026.

Separate research suggests hair loss is often reversible, though experts advise patients to weigh this potential adverse effect against the "substantial and well-established benefits" of GLP-1 therapies for obesity and diabetes.
title: Weight loss drugs linked to increased risk of hair loss, researchers say

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the referral as political theater, leaning on dismissive phrases like “laughably weak” and “meaningless” to portray GOP action as flimsy and performative, while right-leaning coverage casts it as a forceful escalation, using charged terms like “hit with,” “criminal referral,” and “toward prosecution” to stress punishment and accountability.

The key dividing line is whether Jack Smith ’s testimony is seen as a dubious partisan attack or as evidence-backed misconduct; the center stays more procedural, noting possible prosecution and “false statements” without the sharper edge.
Both sides agree on the referral itself, but they diverge over its significance, reflecting broader institutional-versus-partisan priorities.
summary: House Judiciary Committee Chair Jim Jordan recommended criminal charges against former Special Counsel Jack Smith , alleging he made false statements during congressional testimony.

Jordan's referral to the Justice Department centers on claims that Smith misrepresented his team's access to lawmakers' text messages during investigations related to President Trump. Smith's legal team disputes the allegations.
The criminal referral does not require the Justice Department to file charges but adds to ongoing Republican scrutiny of Smith's handling of the Trump investigations.
title: Jordan accuses Smith of lying to Congress in criminal referral to DOJ

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Manila meeting as a cautious “new opening” and a test of whether “stalled diplomacy” can be revived, spotlighting peace-making over confrontation; right-leaning coverage is similar on substance but shifts to broader “peace efforts” and “potential cooperation,” sounding more restrained and regionally expansive.

The sharpest dividing line is whether the story is mainly about Ukraine diplomacy or a wider U.S.- Russia /Asia security agenda, since center/right adds South China Sea and ASEAN context while the left de-emphasizes it.
Shared emphasis on Rubio-Lavrov talks and ending the war reveals a common diplomatic baseline beneath different geopolitical priorities.
summary: On Thursday, State Marco Rubio is set to meet Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov in Manila, Philippines, where he expects to explore renewing United States efforts to broker an end to the war in Ukraine.

Diplomatic efforts to mediate the Russia-Ukraine conflict have stalled over the past several months, but Rubio suggested that new circumstances could create an opening to discuss potential pathways toward a settlement.

Moscow claims 'Anchorage agreements' emerged from President Donald Trump's August 2025 summit with Russian President Vladimir Putin, though Rubio disputes that any formal agreements concerning Ukraine were reached.

Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov indicated he intends to question Rubio about Trump's recent assessment that a settlement is "closer than people realize," as both sides manage areas of disagreement.
Rubio met with Chinese Foreign Minister Wang to discuss regional territorial disputes involving China, as ongoing ASEAN negotiations for a non-aggression pact in the South China Sea continue.
title: Rubio to Revive Prospect of US Helping End War in Ukraine in Meeting with Russian Counterpart

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Hilton’s bid as a Trump-shadowed effort to “sway” or make a “case” to “anti-Trump” voters, stressing his Trump endorsement and his attempt to distance himself from national politics while appealing to Californians “frustrated” with the state.
Right-Leaning coverage instead spotlights a “provocative plea,” using phrases like “You have a choice” and foregrounding the ad itself, turning the message into a sharper, more confrontational pitch.
The key dividing line is whether the story is about Trump backlash or campaign strategy.
Both sides agree he is courting anti-Trump voters, but they portray that outreach through different ideological lenses.
summary: On Wednesday, California Republican gubernatorial candidate Steve Hilton launched his general election campaign in Los Angeles, pitching affordability measures to anti-Trump voters in the heavily Democratic state.

In a state where Democrats outnumber registered Republicans nearly 2-to-1, Hilton's campaign seeks to give voters a "permission slip" to look past partisan labels while maintaining endorsement from President Donald Trump .

At a Boyle Heights warehouse fire site Hilton called a "symbol of Democrat failure," he promised to cut gas prices to $3 a gallon, halve utility bills, and eliminate state income taxes on the first $150,000 in earnings.

Becerra's campaign dismissed the bid, while spokesman Jonathan Underland criticized Hilton for "carrying water" for Trump, citing illegal tariffs that cost California families more than $2,500 this year.
Polling shows Becerra leading 61% to 36% among likely voters, presenting Hilton with a significant uphill battle in the November election as he attempts to overcome the Democratic stronghold.
title: Republican Hilton tells Californians they can hate Trump but still vote for him in governor’s race

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the shooting as a story of ICE overreach, spotlighting allegations that agents “rammed” the van, that the driver “never threatened” them, and even that an officer “switched guns,” while right-leaning coverage narrows the dispute to witness testimony that officers were “not in danger” and the driver was merely “parking,” using that as a quiet rebuttal to alarmist interpretations.

The center sits between them, stressing that statements “contradict” the government’s account and adding the family’s call for “justice.” Both sides agree on the death and the disputed threat level, but diverge sharply over whether the incident signals misconduct or a challenged enforcement narrative.
summary: On July 7, an Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent fatally shot 52-year-old Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston. ICE claims the officer acted in self-defense after being rammed; two passengers maintain agents were never in front of the vehicle.

According to Assistant Attorney General Aaron Reitz, federal officers were investigating two Guatemalan men in a similar vehicle when the pursuit began. Court filings show Salgado Araujo and his passengers had no criminal records.

Passengers wrote in court filings that agents "suddenly cornered us" and fired while the van was stopped. Agents lacked body cameras, and experts noted vehicle damage patterns suggest officers were not in harm's way.

Three witnesses are held in immigration detention, filing habeas corpus petitions for release. A federal judge on Monday barred the government from deporting them or transferring them outside the region without court approval.

This shooting, one of at least 10 during recent enforcement operations, has sparked protests and renewed scrutiny of ICE's use of unmarked vehicles. Border czar Tom Homan said he wants body cameras for all officers "as soon as possible.
title: Passengers in Texas ICE shooting say officers not in danger, driver was parking when shots fired

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as an escalating conflict over alleged AI theft, stressing words like “stole,” “fires back,” and “escalating,” and foregrounding China’s rebuttal as an “unfounded smear”; right-leaning coverage sharpens the blame, using phrases like “large-scale,” “plot,” “tip of China’s spear,” and even “stole” as a settled verdict, while tying Moonshot to U.S.-China AI rivalry and export-control evasion through Nvidia chips.

Both sides center the White House accusation and the broader tech-security dispute, but the left leans toward contested claims and institutional tension, while the right leans toward threat, nationalism, and strategic confrontation.
summary: A White House official accused Moonshot AI of distilling Anthropic 's Fable model to develop its K3 model.
The official also alleged that Moonshot AI acquired banned Nvidia chips and accessed them in Thailand to train its AI models, potentially violating U.S. export controls.
The White House stated that sanctions and export restrictions could be imposed on companies found to be stealing intellectual property from U.S. firms.
title: White House official accuses Chinese startup of distilling Anthropic model, accessing banned Nvidia chips

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the rally as a spectacle of ridicule, spotlighting Trump being “mocked ruthlessly,” “messed up” on his punchline, and making “bats--- crazy” or “wildly” alarmist claims about “communists”; right-leaning coverage instead treats the same event as a forceful campaign moment, elevating anti-communist warnings, “common sense,” and even softer images like Trump with a child, while describing Ossoff with jabs like “Pee-wee Herman look-alike.” The key dividing lines are Trump’s communism rhetoric, the Ossoff insult, and the viral mocking behind him.
Both sides agree the rally centered on Georgia politics , but they diverge sharply on whether it reads as chaos, comedy, or conviction.
summary: On Wednesday, President Donald Trump attacked Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Georgia, calling him a "Democrat communist" and mistakenly comparing him to "Pinky Herman" instead of Pee-wee Herman.
Republicans are targeting the Georgia seat in November to maintain control of the Democratic-held Senate, viewing the Peach State as a key opportunity to flip a seat won by Democrats in 2020.

Trump endorsed Mike Collins, stating, "He's going to beat Pee-wee Herman," while arguing the United States must avoid "communism" to remain strong, claiming that "everything turns to shit" under that system.

Following Trump's July 16 remarks warning of election vulnerabilities, he alleged without evidence that China influenced the 2020 election, downplaying the issue by stating, "We do things to them, too."
The FBI continues to investigate Georgia's 2020 election results, as Trump has long baselessly claimed he won the state, which remains a battleground for both parties in November.
title: Trump rails against Ossoff and ‘Democrat communists’ in Georgia speech

bias: Left-Leaning coverage turns Amish Shah’s primary victory into a vivid rebuke of the “Democratic establishment,” using terms like “suffers,” “preferred choice,” and “left-wing challenger” to spotlight an intraparty power shift and broader anti-insider politics ; center-leaning coverage is cooler, calling it a “blow to the party establishment” while adding that Shah is “moderate” and pointing to the general-election matchup with Jay Feely.
The biggest dividing line is whether the result is a symbolic defeat for party elites or a routine nomination in a key battleground.
Both sides agree Shah won the Arizona seat and beat a party-backed candidate, but they frame the same win through different ideological lenses.
summary: On Tuesday, July 21, 2026, former state Rep. Amish Shah won the Democratic primary for Arizona 's 1st Congressional District, defeating Marlene Galán-Woods; The Associated Press called the race on Wednesday.
National Democratic groups spent millions to support Marlene Galán-Woods against Amish Shah, turning the primary into a bitter contest that tested the influence of the national party establishment.

Outside groups spent more than $1.5 million opposing Amish Shah, compared with roughly $150,000 opposing Marlene Galán-Woods, while Shah's campaign called attacks a "desperate attempt to mislead voters about Amish's record."

Amish Shah will now face Republican Jay Feely, a NFL kicker endorsed by President Donald Trump, in the November 3, 2026 general election while seeking support from the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee.

The 1st Congressional District is a battleground that fell narrowly to Republicans in 2024, when incumbent Republican Representative David Schweikert defeated Amish Shah by a roughly 52-48% margin, making the seat crucial for House control.
title: Amish Shah beats Democratic establishment pick in House primary in Arizona

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as Trump’s coalition fracturing, using phrases like “plummets,” “soured,” and “maximum frustration” to spotlight MAGA backlash and even an insider’s “politically impossible” warning.

Right-Leaning coverage is more conflicted: it also stresses collapsing support, but widens the lens with war-duration, “death toll,” and a “ceasefire collapsed” context, while harsher phrases like “nightmare” and “brutal verdict” turn the conflict into a national crisis.
The key dividing line is whether the polling is treated mainly as Trump’s political damage or as evidence of a deeper, worsening war problem.
Both agree support is eroding and Trump is disputing it, reflecting broader partisan and geopolitical fault lines.
summary: A Politico poll on Wednesday revealed support for the Iran war among self-identified MAGA voters dropped to 37 percent, down from 50 percent in May, creating a growing political liability for President Donald Trump .

The conflict began in February 2026, coinciding with rising inflation and fuel costs that have frequently exceeded $4 per gallon, complicating the administration's economic narrative as the war drags on without resolution.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate appropriations committee on Tuesday that the war's estimated cost reached $37.5 billion, while 65 percent of Americans now favor a deal to end the conflict quickly.

Senior officials describe achieving victory as a "politically impossible situation," according to a source close to the White House, as the administration faces intense pressure following recent combat deaths.
Three months from midterm elections, analysts warn the war's unpopularity and economic impact pose significant risks for Trump, as nearly one in five MAGA supporters now favor immediate withdrawal.
title: Support for Iran war among Trump voters plummets: Poll

summary: The U.S. is shifting its annual $200 billion federal R&D funding away from funding colleges and legacy institutions towards individual scientists instead.
One of the flagship projects is the Genesis Mission, an AI for Science initiative introduced by Trump in late 2025, which aims to double the productivity of U.S. foundational research through AI.

The administration argues that excessive bureaucracy at government agencies and universities is holding the U.S. back, and individual researchers will receive funding directly, minimizing university involvement.
title: Trump administration to shift R&D funding from academia to individual scientists

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Mars flyby as a wonder story, leaning on words like “beautiful,” “incredible,” and “rarely viewed” to spotlight visual novelty and scientific awe, while right-leaning coverage is shorter and more restrained, using phrases like “spectacular” and “new Mars photos” to emphasize a noteworthy NASA update without dwelling on spectacle.
The biggest dividing line is the center’s technical emphasis on “gravity assist,” “asteroid-bound,” precise dates, and instrument testing—details largely de-emphasized on both sides.
All three agree the Psyche mission used Mars as a slingshot toward 16 Psyche, but they diverge on whether the story is chiefly about wonder, mission mechanics, or concise announcement.
summary: NASA released new images on July 17 showing Mars' southern highlands captured by the Psyche spacecraft during a May flyby. The photos feature the 71-mile-wide crater Fournier and the cliff system Oenotria Scopuli.

The probe used Mars' gravity to gain speed while adjusting its trajectory on the 2.2 billion-mile journey to 16 Psyche. Traveling at approximately 12,333 mph upon reaching Mars, the spacecraft executed the maneuver to save propellant.

Approaching within 2200 miles of Mars, the mission calibrated the magnetometer and multispectral imager. Jim Bell, Psyche imager instrument lead at Arizona State University, said the results offer "rarely seen views of the red planet."
Bob Mase, the mission's project manager at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, confirmed the maneuver was executed perfectly. The spacecraft is now on track to rendezvous with 16 Psyche in 2029.

Scientists aim to study 16 Psyche, which contains about 1% of the main asteroid belt's mass, to gain insights into Earth's own core. The mission will differentiate between the asteroid's metallic and silicate components.
title: NASA's Psyche Spacecraft Captured a Time-Lapse Video of Its Mars Flyby

bias: The left frames the case as a restrained legal update, emphasizing the 27-month sentence and the woman’s “threatening” messages, while the center and right sharpen the threat narrative with phrases like “death threats,” “repeatedly,” and “threatening to kill,” making the conduct feel more severe.

The center adds defendant details, a guilty plea, and supervised release, signaling a fuller case-context lens; the right broadens into a wider public-safety frame, even tacking on a separate threats case involving Jews and police .
Both sides converge on the prison sentence and Nancy Mace’s Republican status, reflecting deeper divides over criminality, political identity, and threat framing.
summary: Two individuals received prison sentences after pleading guilty in separate cases involving threats against U.S. Rep. Nancy Mace .
A federal court sentenced two defendants in separate cases for making threats against Rep. Nancy Mace, according to court records.
Two defendants were sentenced after being convicted in separate cases involving threats directed at Rep. Nancy Mace.
title: 2 people sentenced to prison for threatening Mace’s life

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the poll as a symptom of broader social breakdown, using phrases like “shared anxieties,” “combative,” and “vitriolic polarization” to stress emotional strain, plus context such as politics ranking below road accidents and crime.

Right-Leaning coverage strips the story to a sharper warning, leaning on “greatest risk” and “personal safety” to portray politics as a direct threat, while de-emphasizing the wider polarization narrative.
The pivot points are the 10% figure, the rise from 6%, and the U.S. leading 140 countries: left treats them as evidence of deepening division, right as proof of danger.
Both agree the result signals a worsening political climate.
summary: According to the 2026 Lloyd's Register Foundation World Risk Poll, 10% of Americans identify politics as their greatest personal safety risk, up from 6% in 2021 and 2023.

The United States statistically ties with Hungary at 9% for the highest percentage of adults identifying politics as a top safety concern, representing 10 times the global average of 1% across 140 countries.

Although road-related accidents and crime rank as higher threats at 34% and 19% respectively, political concerns appear uniform across demographics, with similar percentages of Republicans, Democrats, and independents citing politics as a top risk.

Deep distrust persists, with 58% of Americans saying the government ignores their well-being, a sharp rise from 47% in 2021, as respondents variously blamed political extremism, polarization, or specific administrations.

Polarization may reflect broader existential anxiety, where citizens fear the "other side" represents a real and impending threat to their existence, linked to poor mental health and increased aggression.
title: More Americans identify politics as ‘greatest source of risk’ to their safety: Gallup
FCC votes to bar sales of devices with parts from Chinese firms posing risks
US FDA says it is investigating another cyclosporiasis outbreak

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the proposal cautiously as nuclear plants in “ocean waters,” stressing the novelty and implicit risk, while right-leaning coverage recasts it as a large-scale opportunity in “federal waters,” spotlighting the vast “3.2 billion acres” and the administration’s “exploring” posture.

The rhetoric splits between tentative, measured wording like “weighs” on the left and more promotive phrases on the right such as “potential” and “speed deployment.” The biggest dividing lines are scale, oversight, and feasibility: the center alone notes no approved U.S. project and cites Russia’s floating reactor as context.
Both sides, however, share the core theme of expanding domestic nuclear energy.
summary: On Wednesday, the Marine Minerals Administration and the Nuclear Regulatory Commission announced an agreement to cooperate on evaluating potential undersea nuclear power plants in federal waters.
President Donald Trump aims to quadruple domestic nuclear capacity by 2050, while Long Beach-based Bluecore Energy develops small modular reactors for potential maritime applications.

Submerged reactor systems have been used safely in naval applications for 70 years, MMA Acting Director Matt Giacona said, noting the agreement is designed to assess if the technology could be "responsibly implemented."

The administration is exploring projects across 3.2 billion acres of federal waters, though officials noted no commercial plants are currently planned, distinguishing this from recent efforts to block offshore wind farms.

Environmental group Center for Biological Diversity campaigner Nick Katkevich called nuclear reactors on old oil platforms one of the administration's "worst" ideas amid broader efforts to open 31 million acres off American Samoa for seabed mining.
title: Trump administration considers putting nuclear power plants in US waters

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a straightforward corporate move, stressing Amazon ’s AGI unit and its broader remit, like Nova models and even silicon/quantum work, while right-leaning coverage turns the same cuts into a sharper cautionary tale, using phrases like “fresh job cuts,” “layoffs,” and “latest selective workforce reduction” to spotlight instability and the shadow of an earlier “16,000-employee” round.
The center adds a strategic gloss—“sharpens its AI focus” and “customer-facing AI”—that softens the labor hit into a business pivot.
Across both sides, the dividing line is whether the emphasis falls on restructuring or on job loss, even as all agree Amazon is refocusing its AI effort.
summary: On Wednesday, Amazon confirmed layoffs within its AGI unit, marking the latest round of workforce reductions as the company prioritizes generative AI projects and infrastructure development.
Amazon stated it is "sharpening our focus on the initiatives that matter most for customers," describing the cuts as difficult decisions needed to move faster on what counts most.
Teams under AGI Data Services and AGI Information were impacted, with affected U.S. employees receiving 90 days of pay and benefits, plus outplacement support and severance eligibility.
These reductions follow a broader trend; Amazon has eliminated more than 30,000 positions since last October while simultaneously investing tens of billions in AI infrastructure.
Despite these personnel changes, Amazon remains committed to advancing AGI research and competing with industry leaders OpenAI and Anthropic in developing transformative AI technologies.
title: Amazon cuts jobs in its artificial general intelligence group

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Woolsey through turmoil and betrayal, stressing his “embattled” CIA tenure, the “traitor” Aldrich Ames scandal, and even Havana syndrome, while right-leaning coverage pivots to legacy and ideology, calling him a “hawk,” highlighting his later support for Trump, his Newsmax role, and claims he “falsely denounced” Saddam’s WMD.
The center stays with a “complex political legacy” and the stroke death notice.

Pivotal dividing lines are whether his career is read as institutional crisis or national-security service, and both sides share the obituary basics but diverge on whether his story signals turbulence, caution, or conservative realignment.
summary: Former CIA Director James Woolsey died Tuesday at his Washington home at age 84, having led the agency during a turbulent tenure under President Bill Clinton.
His wife, Conchita Sarnoff Woolsey, told newspapers he died from a stroke after his health declined in recent years due to "Havana syndrome," a mysterious condition affecting diplomats.
Before leading the CIA, Woolsey served as undersecretary of the Navy under President Carter and as special negotiator for President George Bush on the Conventional Armed Forces in Europe Treaty.
During his 1993–1995 CIA tenure, Woolsey faced criticism over the Aldrich Ames espionage case; Ames admitted being paid $2.5 million by Moscow for U.S. secrets.
Woolsey cautioned against overreacting to security vulnerabilities, warning that strict measures could create "a mood or attitude of paranoia and false accusations" damaging agency morale.
title: James Woolsey, CIA director under Bill Clinton, dies at 84

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Mali as another chapter in Trump’s escalating pattern, using charged phrases like “attack,” “risky strikes,” and “mired in a war” to stress danger, overreach, and domestic fallout, including his slipping support among Republicans.
Right-Leaning coverage pulls back, foregrounding that action is merely “considered” and stressing “no decision yet,” with a cooler, cautionary tone.
The center adds geopolitical context by noting France and Russia have “tried” for years, implying a stubborn problem rather than pure aggression.
All sides agree the target is JNIM, but they split sharply over whether this signals prudent security policy or reckless militarism.
summary: The Trump administration is preparing plans to strike JNIM, an al-Qaeda-aligned militant group operating from Mali, according to The Washington Post. If approved, Mali would become the eighth nation where President Donald Trump has authorized military operations since taking office.

Operating from Mali, JNIM, or Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin, has transformed into the region's most formidable militant force and expanded across coastal West African nations. The group now ranks among the world's most heavily armed insurgencies.

Mali, a landlocked nation of nearly 25 million, has in recent years been plagued by violence from Islamist militant groups, the junta government, and Russian mercenaries. Any direct U.S. military involvement risks confrontation with Russian forces, requiring deconfliction efforts between Washington and Moscow akin to those in Syria.

Already mired in the Iran war that has eroded support among the Republican Party , President Donald Trump faces internal divisions over the Mali proposal. National Security Council counterterrorism director Sebastian Gorka pushes for military action while others urge caution.

Approving Mali strikes would mark Trump's eighth authorized military operation, directly contradicting his campaign pledge to end America's endless wars. The proposal has sparked intense internal debate among senior officials over whether to authorize the strikes amid competing political and strategic pressures.
title: Trump mulling military strikes on Mali militant group: Report

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event as a troubling police killing that “sparked protests,” using terms like “fatal,” “kills,” and “accountability” to stress harm and scrutiny, while right-leaning coverage leans into justification, foregrounding “resisting arrest,” “armed with knife,” and “police say” to cast the officer’s actions as defensive and the backlash as a secondary consequence.
The biggest dividing line is whether the story is about police violence and public outcry or an enforcement encounter gone violent.

Center coverage stays more procedural with “death investigation” and “what we know.” All sides agree on the shooting, knife claim, video, and protests—mirroring deeper tensions over policing, protest politics, and institutional trust.
summary: A Madison police officer shot and killed a man who had injured him with a knife while resisting arrest, Madison Police Chief John Patterson said Wednesday. The man died later at the hospital.

Police responded to reports of someone entering parked vehicles before the suspect fled on a bike through nearby backyards, Patterson said. During the struggle, the man pulled a knife and injured an officer.

An officer deployed a taser but it was unsuccessful before the veteran officer fired his weapon. The shooting occurred on a city street near the Capitol, where numerous cars stopped as the event unfolded.

The Wisconsin Department of Criminal Investigation will examine the case because it involves police. Brandi Grayson, head of the Madison-based Black community advocacy group Urban Triage, stated, "Another Black man is dead."

A police officer shot and killed Tony Robinson on the same street in 2015, a shooting that spurred large protests. While police did not release the race of those involved, advocates identified the man killed as Black.
title: Wisconsin police officer shoots, kills man on city street near state Capitol in Madison

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the strikes as an escalation with humanitarian stakes, emphasizing “civilian infrastructure,” “regional turmoil,” and the warning that “no infrastructure will be safe,” while right-leaning coverage spotlights deterrence, toughness, and wider fallout, using sharper language like “JUST IN,” “fresh wave,” “open the gates of hell,” and “firing line.” The dividing lines are clear: the left foregrounds mutual civilian risk, the center stresses the stated U.S. rationale of “further degrade” and protecting mariners, and the right broadens the story into oil, allies, and regional crisis.
All sides agree the 12th night of strikes signals worsening U.S.- Iran confrontation.
summary: On Wednesday, United States Central Command announced a 12th night of strikes on Iran, targeting military infrastructure to degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels in regional waters.

Diplomatic efforts remain stalled as Iran's foreign minister, Seyed Abbas Araghchi, declared an "eye for an eye" defense doctrine on Wednesday, warning that any aggression against Iranian infrastructure will compel a "powerful and decisive response."
Iran's Health Ministry reports 53 deaths and nearly 600 injuries from 11 days of strikes, while Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth estimated the United States has spent $37.5 billion on the war so far.

President Donald Trump warned Wednesday that the United States will destroy "one bridge or power plant" each time Iran shoots at a ship, following missile attacks that triggered alerts in Jordan, Bahrain, and Saudi Arabia.

Rubio noted the United States remains open to negotiations, but "right now they don't seem to be serious about that," while warning against creating precedents where nations control international waterways and demand tolls.
title: US military says it's carrying out its 12th night of strikes on Iran as both sides threaten civilian infrastructure

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes escalation and institutional strain, using alarmed phrases like “off the charts” and “highest we’ve ever seen” to frame the Secret Service as confronting an unprecedented danger while still noting confidence in the security plan.

Right-Leaning coverage pushes harder into threat-and-disorder rhetoric, leaning on words like “warns,” “highly volatile,” “skyrocketed,” and attention to drone mitigation and tighter protocols, turning the story into a security-crisis narrative.
The key dividing line is how the same 40% rise in threats and “10,000” investigations are cast: as a stark warning, operational challenge, or routine scale.
Both sides agree the risk is unusually high, but they diverge on urgency, context, and institutional vulnerability.
summary: On Wednesday, the U.S. Secret Service reported 10,000 threat cases against government officials in 2026, representing a 40% increase over the same period last year.

Mental health committals for individuals suspected of threatening protectees have spiked by a factor of 10, reflecting broader political violence concerns documented by law enforcement officials in recent years.

The FBI detected 1,600 drones around U.S. stadiums during the World Cup and seized more than 700 illegal drones, with officials warning that consumer drones pose rapidly growing threats to protected venues.

This Friday, President Donald Trump attends the rescheduled White House Correspondents' Association dinner at the Waldorf Astoria, where agents have reevaluated security protocols following an April gunman's attempt to breach the event.
title: Secret Service has investigated 10,000 threats in 2026

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames ICE’s retreat as a rebuke to enforcement expansion, foregrounding “drops plan” and “detention centers” to spotlight the cancellation itself, while the right leans into “giving up” and “hold thousands of people,” a tone that suggests institutional failure and scale.
The center is more explanatory, adding permit denials and Gov.
Josh Shapiro’s “serious risks” language, which shifts the story toward governance and community impact.
The dividing line is whether the move is read as a restraint on detention power or a bureaucratic setback.
All sides agree the plan was abandoned, but they diverge on whether that signals protection, retreat, or administrative consequence.
summary: On Wednesday, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement formally withdrew its appeal of permit denials, abandoning plans to convert two rural Pennsylvania warehouses into detention centers.

The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection had denied essential drinking water and wastewater permits, citing inadequate local infrastructure to support the thousands of inhabitants envisioned by ICE.
Governor Josh Shapiro, a Democrat, argued the warehouses were unsuitable for detainees, warning that conversion would pose serious health and safety risks to surrounding communities.
These warehouses were part of former Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem's $38 billion expansion plan, which her successor, Markwayne Mullin, paused following community criticism last month.
Although ICE confirmed it will not use the properties for detention, the agency has not specified future plans for the sites, which local officials learned of only after acquisitions were made.
title: ICE drops plan to convert 2 Pennsylvania warehouses into detention centers

bias: Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, while center and right diverge sharply: the center frames Mamdani’s Netanyahu criticism as a warning against “erasure” of “Jewish pain,” using a measured, moral-register tone; the right expands the story into a broader indictment, branding him an “Israel-first” problem, a purveyor of “failed communist ideas,” and a figure who “dodges” and “draws equivalence” on “genocide.” That contrast reveals a key dividing line: whether the issue is respectful language on Tisha B’Av or a wider threat to city leadership, taxes , and identity politics.
Both sides nonetheless converge on one point—Mamdani’s Israel-related remarks are politically combustible and tied to deeper debates over Jewish memory and New York priorities.
summary: On Tuesday, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani described Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu as a "war criminal" and the "architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people."
Rabbi David Katz, executive director of the Israel Heritage Foundation, previously urged New Yorkers to respect the mayor after his January inauguration but reversed course following the remarks.
Katz said he was "very shocked" by the comments, arguing they were unworthy of the office of mayor and demanding an apology to all New Yorkers.
City Hall did not immediately respond to the criticism; Katz called for the mayor's resignation, arguing political disagreements should not prevent recognizing an elected leader.
The incident highlights ongoing tensions regarding the conflict in Gaza, as community leaders debate whether criticism of the Israeli government constitutes rejection of the community's belonging.
title: Rabbi David Katz Reverses Course on Mamdani, Calls on NYC Mayor to Resign Over Netanyahu Remarks

bias: The center-leaning coverage stays tightly focused on Danon’s rebuke and Eisenkot’s sharp judgment, framing Mamdani’s “war criminal” label through terms like “blood libels,” “dangerous,” “inflammatory,” and “pure antisemitism,” while the right-leaning coverage widens the attack into a larger indictment, using phrases like “sleazeball,” “ Hamas propaganda,” and “political spectacle” to cast Mamdani as deceptive and performative.
A key dividing line is scale and intent: the right emphasizes millions of views, Jewish-rights-group condemnation, and “Oct. 7 revisionism,” whereas the center keeps the dispute more contained.
Both converge on Israel, antisemitism, and Netanyahu, reflecting a broader ideological clash over protest, legitimacy, and political framing.
summary: On Tuesday, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani posted a video calling Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu a "war criminal" and urging his arrest if he visits the city, citing his responsibility to address human rights violations.

Citing the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant, Mamdani acknowledged New York lacks independent legal authority to enforce it, though he had promised during his 2025 campaign to pursue Netanyahu's detention if elected.

Former IDF chief of staff Gadi Eisenkot and former Prime Minister Naftali Bennett condemned the remarks as "pure antisemitism," asserting the mayor failed to acknowledge the October 7 Hamas-led massacre that triggered the war.

President Donald Trump stated this week that Netanyahu will not be arrested while visiting the United States, while Ambassador to the United Nations Danny Danon accused Mamdani of promoting "Hamas propaganda" instead of serving constituents.

Netanyahu is expected to visit New York for the United Nations General Assembly in September, as the confrontation underscores ongoing tensions between Mamdani's administration and Israeli political leaders over the war in Gaza.
title: 'Enough with the Blood Libels': Danon Decries Mamdani's Labeling of Netanyahu as War Criminal

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the museum dispute as a rights-based defense of a Palestinian exhibit, stressing “Nakba,” “withdraw criticism,” and even “pro-Israel lobby groups” to cast opposition as pressure on historical recognition; center coverage is more procedural, noting a “torrent of criticism” and the museum’s federal status without the activist edge.

Right-Leaning coverage pivots away from the exhibit entirely, using hard-edged terms like “terrorist murderers,” “Pride,” and “Heroic” to condemn Palestinian Authority-linked figures and spotlight violence.
Both sides circle the same broader fault line—Israel-Palestine memory politics —but diverge on whether the story is about marginalized history or extremist glorification.
summary: On Wednesday, a coalition of civil society groups issued a joint statement supporting The Canadian Museum of Human Rights, pushing back against criticism of its exhibit on Palestinian displacement.
The Winnipeg-based museum has faced significant criticism over its exhibit on the Nakba, the forced displacement of about 750,000 Palestinians during the 1948 Arab-Israeli war.
Culture Minister Marc Miller claimed the museum failed by not naming Hamas as a terrorist organization, while Jewish groups argue the exhibit portrays only one side of a complex history.
Organizations including Amnesty International, the United Church, and the Union of National Employees are calling on Ottawa to protect the museum's curatorial independence.
These groups characterize the criticism as an organized pressure campaign, urging Ottawa to defend the museum against what they view as political interference in its operations.
title: Coalition calls on minister to withdraw criticism of museum’s Palestinian exhibit
GOP senator blocks Schumer request to stop Trump from using taxpayer money to ‘max out’ new Air Force One
First lady Melania Trump files motion for sanctions against Michael Wolff over tossed lawsuit
House Votes to Adopt Department of War Renaming in Annual Defense Bill
